title: Postoperative Care: Total Hip Replacement
scenario:
Mrs. Eleanor Hayes is a 74-year-old female admitted to the orthopedic surgical unit on postoperative day (POD) #1 following a right total hip arthroplasty performed for severe osteoarthritis. Her past medical history includes osteoporosis, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Current medications include metoprolol 25mg BID, lisinopril 10mg daily, metformin (held perioperatively), and enoxaparin 40mg subcutaneously daily for DVT prophylaxis.

On your 1200 assessment, you obtain the following vital signs: BP 102/64 mmHg, HR 112 bpm (regular), RR 20 breaths/min, Temperature 38.4°C (101.1°F), and SpO2 94% on 2L nasal cannula. The patient reports pain 8/10 at the right hip incision site. The Jackson-Pratt (JP) drain has collected 50mL of serosanguineous drainage over the past 4 hours. Morning laboratory results reveal a hemoglobin of 7.8 g/dL, compared to a preoperative hemoglobin of 12.4 g/dL — a drop of 4.6 g/dL. Urine output has been 20mL/hour for the past two consecutive hours, well below the minimum acceptable threshold of 30mL/hour. The patient is noted to be confused and is pulling at her IV line. The nursing assistant reports she was oriented earlier this morning.

title: Sepsis Recognition and Management
scenario: A 58-year-old male is admitted to the medical-surgical unit from the emergency department with a 3-day history of productive cough and fever. His past medical history is significant for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and alcohol use disorder. On admission, vital signs are: Temperature 39.2°C (102.6°F), Heart Rate 118 beats/min, Blood Pressure 88/54 mmHg, Respiratory Rate 26 breaths/min, and SpO2 89% on room air. Laboratory results reveal WBC 18,400/mm³, serum lactate 3.8 mmol/L, procalcitonin elevated above normal limits, and creatinine 1.9 mg/dL (baseline 0.9 mg/dL). Two sets of blood cultures have been drawn. The patient is currently receiving supplemental oxygen via 4L nasal cannula and a 1-liter normal saline bolus is infusing. He is confused and unable to follow complex commands. His skin is mottled over bilateral lower extremities. Urine output over the past 2 hours has been only 35 mL. The nurse recognizes a critical clinical picture requiring immediate systematic intervention to prevent end-organ failure and death.

title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) Management
scenario: A 22-year-old female with a known history of Type 1 diabetes mellitus is brought to the emergency department by her roommate after being found confused and lethargic at home. The roommate reports the patient has not taken insulin for approximately two days, stating she 'forgot.' The patient has experienced nausea, vomiting, and diffuse abdominal pain for the past 24 hours with minimal oral intake. On arrival, the patient is visibly ill and diaphoretic. Vital signs: BP 96/58 mmHg, HR 126 bpm (regular), RR 28 breaths/min (deep and labored — Kussmaul respirations), Temperature 37.1°C, SpO2 98% on room air. A distinctive fruity odor is noted on the patient's breath. GCS is 13 (confused, follows commands). Point-of-care glucose reads 624 mg/dL. Stat laboratory results reveal: pH 7.18, bicarbonate 9 mEq/L, potassium 5.8 mEq/L, sodium 128 mEq/L, anion gap 26, and large ketones on urinalysis. IV access is established and fluid resuscitation is initiated. The team prepares to manage this patient for diabetic ketoacidosis.

title: Acute Decompensated Heart Failure
scenario: Mr. Harold Webb is a 67-year-old male with a history of ischemic cardiomyopathy and a left ventricular ejection fraction (EF) of 25%. His home medications include furosemide 40mg daily and carvedilol 12.5mg BID. Over the past three days, he has gained 8 pounds despite taking his medications. He presents to the emergency department with dyspnea at rest and orthopnea, reporting he has been sleeping in a recliner for the past two nights. On assessment, the nurse notes bilateral crackles extending to the mid-lung fields, 3+ pitting edema to the bilateral knees, and an irregular heart rhythm. Vital signs: BP 168/94 mmHg, HR 102 bpm (atrial fibrillation on telemetry), RR 28 breaths/min, SpO2 88% on room air — improving to 93% on 4L nasal cannula. Laboratory results reveal BNP 2,840 pg/mL and creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (baseline 1.2 mg/dL). Chest X-ray demonstrates pulmonary edema and cardiomegaly. The provider orders IV furosemide 80mg and morphine 2mg IVP has been administered. The patient is admitted to the cardiac step-down unit for management of acute decompensated heart failure.

title: Acute Ischemic Stroke: tPA Decision and Nursing Care
scenario: A 61-year-old female is brought to the emergency department by EMS after her husband witnessed a sudden onset of right-sided facial droop, right arm drift, and slurred speech approximately 90 minutes ago. Last known well time is confirmed at 90 minutes prior to arrival, with onset-to-door time of 75 minutes. On arrival, the patient is alert but dysarthric and unable to follow complex commands. Neurological assessment reveals a right facial droop, right upper extremity drift with pronation, and garbled speech. The NIHSS score is calculated at 14, indicating a moderate-to-severe stroke. Vital signs: BP 188/104 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, RR 16 breaths/min, Temp 36.8°C, SpO2 96% on room air. Emergent non-contrast CT head shows no hemorrhage and no early ischemic changes. Laboratory results: glucose 134 mg/dL, INR 1.1. The patient is not on anticoagulants, has no history of hemorrhagic stroke, and has undergone no recent surgery. A bedside dysphagia screen is performed — the patient fails the water swallow test, indicating dysphagia. The stroke team is evaluating the patient as a tPA candidate. IV access is established and the team prepares for rapid decision-making.

title: Pediatric Asthma: Acute Severe Exacerbation
scenario:
An 8-year-old male (weight 28 kg) is brought to the emergency department by his mother after developing acute respiratory distress following exposure to a neighbor's dog. He has a documented history of moderate persistent asthma and is prescribed daily inhaled fluticasone/salmeterol for maintenance therapy. His mother administered albuterol MDI 2 puffs approximately 30 minutes prior to arrival with no improvement in symptoms.

On arrival, he is visibly distressed, sitting in tripod position, and speaking only in 2-3 word sentences. Vital signs: respiratory rate 36 breaths/min, heart rate 128 bpm, blood pressure 98/62 mmHg, temperature 37.2°C, SpO2 88% on room air. Physical assessment reveals nasal flaring, suprasternal and intercostal retractions, and active use of accessory muscles. Auscultation reveals diffuse expiratory wheezing bilaterally. Peak flow measurement is 42% of his personal best, confirming severe exacerbation per NAEPP guidelines (< 40% = severe).

The clinical picture is consistent with an acute severe asthma exacerbation requiring immediate escalation of bronchodilator therapy, systemic anti-inflammatory intervention, and supplemental oxygen. Close monitoring for respiratory failure is essential, as his work of breathing is significant and his oxygenation is critically low.

title: Acute Kidney Injury: Recognition and Management
scenario: Mr. Harold Jennings is a 71-year-old male admitted to the surgical progressive care unit on post-operative day 2 following an emergency bowel resection for perforated diverticulitis. He received gentamicin and vancomycin for perioperative infection coverage. His baseline creatinine was 1.1 mg/dL on admission. Over the past 48 hours, creatinine has risen to 1.9 mg/dL and now 3.4 mg/dL. Yesterday his urine output was 45 mL/hr; today it has dropped to 12 mL/hr over the past four hours. Current labs reveal BUN 58 mg/dL, potassium 5.9 mEq/L, and phosphorus 5.8 mg/dL. His blood pressure is 158/96 mmHg. On assessment, the nurse notes bilateral crackles in the lung bases and documents a weight gain of 3.2 kg since surgery. A bedside bladder scan reveals no retained urine, ruling out obstruction. Current medications include vancomycin (last trough level 28 mcg/mL — supratherapeutic), IV fluids running at 75 mL/hr, and lisinopril 10 mg daily (home medication continued post-op). The ECG monitor now shows peaked T waves. The nurse must act quickly to address this deteriorating clinical picture.

title: Postpartum Hemorrhage: Recognition and Emergency Management
scenario:
Maria G., a 28-year-old G3P3 female, delivered vaginally 45 minutes ago following an 18-hour labor augmented with oxytocin. The newborn weighed 4,200 grams and the placenta was delivered intact with no noted defects. Maria's immediate post-delivery vital signs were BP 118/72, HR 82, RR 16, SpO2 99%. Estimated blood loss at delivery was 350 mL, within normal limits.

The bedside nurse is now called to evaluate heavy vaginal bleeding. Maria has saturated 2 perineal pads within 10 minutes. On assessment, the fundus is boggy and displaced to the right of midline. A bladder scan reveals 450 mL of retained urine. Repeat vital signs show BP 88/54 mmHg, HR 128 bpm, RR 22 breaths/min, and SpO2 95% on room air. Current estimated blood loss is 900 mL. Maria reports feeling dizzy and anxious. Current IV access consists of one 18-gauge peripheral line with an oxytocin infusion running at 125 mL/hr (maintenance dose). The obstetric provider has been paged but has not yet arrived at bedside. The clinical picture is consistent with acute postpartum hemorrhage with early hemorrhagic shock.

title: Mental Health: Suicidal Patient — Safety Assessment and Care
scenario: A 34-year-old male is brought to the emergency department by police after a neighbor called 911. He was found sitting in a running car inside a closed garage. EMS removed him from the scene and administered 100% oxygen via non-rebreather mask. On arrival, his carboxyhemoglobin (CO-Hgb) was 22%; after two hours of 100% oxygen therapy, it has decreased to 8%. The patient is now alert and oriented x4. He states, 'I don't want to be here anymore.' Past medical history is significant for major depressive disorder with two prior suicide attempts: one intentional overdose and one wrist laceration requiring sutures. He is prescribed sertraline 100mg daily but reports he stopped taking it three weeks ago. Psychosocial stressors include divorce finalized six months ago and recent job loss. He denies a current specific plan but admits, 'I've been thinking about it a lot.' There are no signs of psychosis. He is medically cleared pending psychiatric evaluation. Vital signs are stable. The nurse is preparing to conduct a comprehensive suicide risk assessment and implement a safety plan.

title: Community-Acquired Pneumonia with Hypoxic Respiratory Failure
scenario: A 78-year-old female nursing home resident is brought by ambulance to the emergency department with a 3-day history of productive cough, fever, and decreased oral intake. Her past medical history includes COPD (on home oxygen at 2L via nasal cannula), type 2 diabetes mellitus, and mild dementia. On arrival, vital signs are: Temperature 39.6°C, Heart Rate 114 bpm, Blood Pressure 104/62 mmHg, Respiratory Rate 32 breaths/min, and SpO2 82% on 4L nasal cannula (baseline SpO2 91% on 2L at home). She is confused and more disoriented than her baseline. Chest X-ray reveals right lower lobe consolidation. Laboratory results show WBC 22.4 k/µL, procalcitonin 4.8 ng/mL, and lactate 2.4 mmol/L. Arterial blood gas on 4L NC: pH 7.28, PaCO2 58 mmHg, PaO2 52 mmHg, indicating combined hypoxic and hypercapnic respiratory failure with acute respiratory acidosis. Sputum is purulent. Two sets of blood cultures have been drawn and urine Legionella antigen is pending. Empiric treatment with ceftriaxone and azithromycin has been initiated. A Do-Not-Resuscitate / Do-Not-Intubate (DNR/DNI) order is on file per the patient's advance directive. Family members are present at bedside.

title: Hypertensive Emergency: Malignant Hypertension
scenario: A 54-year-old male is brought to the emergency department by his wife after complaining of a severe headache rated 9/10, blurred vision, and nausea for the past several hours. His wife reports he stopped taking his antihypertensive medications — lisinopril and amlodipine — approximately two weeks ago, stating he 'felt fine.' He has a known history of hypertension but no other documented chronic conditions. On arrival, the patient appears confused and disoriented to time and place. Fundoscopic examination reveals bilateral papilledema. Vital signs: BP 228/142 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, RR 18 breaths/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 97% on room air. Labs show creatinine 2.8 mg/dL (baseline unknown) and glucose 118 mg/dL. Urinalysis reveals 3+ proteinuria and RBC casts, consistent with hypertensive nephropathy. ECG shows a left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH) pattern. CT scan of the head shows no hemorrhage and no infarct. The clinical team initiates evaluation for hypertensive emergency with evidence of multi-organ target damage including neurological, renal, and cardiovascular involvement. IV access is established and continuous monitoring is initiated.

title: Acute Severe Pancreatitis: Management and Complications
scenario: A 46-year-old male with a history of heavy alcohol use (12+ drinks/day) is brought to the emergency department by his partner after developing sudden-onset severe epigastric pain rated 9/10, radiating directly through to his back. He reports nausea and has vomited eight times over the past eight hours. He denies recent trauma, new medications, or prior episodes. On assessment, the patient is diaphoretic and appears acutely ill. Vital signs: BP 96/58 mmHg, HR 124 bpm, RR 22 breaths/min, Temperature 38.8°C, SpO2 94% on room air. Abdomen is diffusely tender with guarding; bowel sounds are diminished. Laboratory results reveal lipase 4,820 U/L (normal <60), amylase 1,240 U/L, WBC 18,600/mm³, hematocrit 52% (hemoconcentration), BUN 34 mg/dL, creatinine 1.8 mg/dL, calcium 7.2 mg/dL, and glucose 318 mg/dL. Chest X-ray demonstrates a left pleural effusion. CT abdomen confirms pancreatic inflammation with peripancreatic fat stranding and no evidence of necrosis. RANSON score is calculated at 4, indicating severe acute pancreatitis. The patient is admitted to the ICU for close monitoring and aggressive management.

title: Pediatric Bacterial Meningitis: Emergency Recognition and Care
scenario: A 6-year-old male is brought to the emergency department by his parents with a 12-hour history of high fever, severe headache, and neck stiffness. Over the past hour, he has become increasingly lethargic. His parents report he has been immunized but missed his Hib and MenACWY booster doses. Current vital signs: Temperature 40.1°C, Heart Rate 148 bpm, Blood Pressure 88/52 mmHg, Respiratory Rate 28 breaths/min, SpO2 96% on room air. Weight: 22 kg. Neurological assessment reveals a Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score of 12 (E3V4M5), positive Kernig's sign, positive Brudzinski's sign, and photophobia. On skin assessment, the nurse identifies 3 petechial lesions on the trunk and extremities that appear to be spreading. Two sets of blood cultures have been drawn. A lumbar puncture (LP) has been ordered; results are pending. The child is placed in a room immediately. The nurse recognizes this presentation as a potential life-threatening neurological emergency requiring rapid assessment, isolation precautions, and time-sensitive interventions. The clinical team is coordinating emergent diagnostics and empiric treatment.

title: Post-Cardiac Catheterization: Femoral Access Site Monitoring
scenario: A 62-year-old male is 2 hours post-percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) via right femoral artery access. A drug-eluting stent was successfully placed in the left anterior descending (LAD) artery for a 90% occlusion found on diagnostic angiography. The patient received a heparin drip during the procedure, which was discontinued 30 minutes ago. He was administered aspirin 325mg and ticagrelor 180mg loading dose per post-PCI protocol. Current vital signs: BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 78 bpm (normal sinus rhythm), RR 16 breaths/min, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 98.4°F. Assessment of the right groin reveals manual pressure has been applied; a small hematoma measuring 3x3 cm is noted at the puncture site with no active oozing. Right foot is warm to touch, dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are 2+, and capillary refill is 2 seconds. Pre-procedure creatinine was 1.1 mg/dL; contrast volume used was 180 mL. The patient reports the right leg 'feels numb and tingly.' Urine output for the last hour is 22 mL. The nurse is responsible for post-procedure monitoring and must assess for potential access site and systemic complications.

title: Alcohol Withdrawal Syndrome: Delirium Tremens Management
scenario: A 48-year-old male was admitted 36 hours ago for community-acquired pneumonia and is currently receiving ceftriaxone. Social history reveals a 10-year history of heavy alcohol use, consuming approximately 1 liter of vodka daily. His last drink was on the day of admission. Over the past several hours, the patient has become increasingly agitated, diaphoretic, and tremulous. He is now hallucinating, repeatedly pointing at the walls and shouting about insects crawling on them. Current vital signs: BP 168/104 mmHg, HR 132 bpm, RR 22 breaths/min, Temp 38.6°C, SpO2 96% on room air. Neurological assessment reveals nystagmus and bilaterally dilated pupils. The Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol (CIWA-Ar) score is 28, indicating severe withdrawal. Twenty minutes ago, the patient experienced a new-onset generalized tonic-clonic seizure that self-resolved after 90 seconds. Current medications include ceftriaxone for pneumonia and thiamine 100 mg IV daily (started on admission). Lorazepam 2 mg IV q6h PRN has been ordered; the last dose was administered 5 hours ago. The nurse recognizes this clinical picture as delirium tremens and initiates the alcohol withdrawal protocol.

title: Thyroid Storm: Recognition and Emergency Management
scenario: A 38-year-old female with a known history of Graves' disease presents to the emergency department following an appendectomy performed yesterday. Her family reports she stopped taking propylthiouracil (PTU) approximately 3 weeks ago because she "felt better." The surgical stress appears to have precipitated a thyroid crisis. On assessment, the patient is agitated, confused, and diaphoretic. She has vomited twice since arrival. Vital signs: Temperature 40.8°C (105.4°F), Heart Rate 168 bpm with atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (afib with RVR) on telemetry, Blood Pressure 158/72 mmHg (wide pulse pressure of 86), Respiratory Rate 28 breaths/min, SpO2 96% on room air. Laboratory results reveal TSH <0.01 mIU/L (critically low) and free T4 at a critically elevated level. The Burch-Wartofsky Point Scale score is calculated at 65, indicating thyroid storm is likely. Two large-bore IV lines have been established. A cooling blanket is in place. The physician has been notified and is at bedside. The nurse prepares to implement emergency thyroid storm management protocol.

title: COPD Acute Exacerbation with Hypercapnic Respiratory Failure
scenario: A 69-year-old male with GOLD Stage III COPD presents to the ED with a 4-day history of worsening dyspnea, increased sputum production (yellow-green), and low-grade fever. He is a known chronic CO2 retainer with a baseline PaCO2 of 58 mmHg and pH 7.36 (compensated respiratory acidosis). He uses home oxygen at 2L via nasal cannula. On arrival, SpO2 is 84% on his home 2L NC; triage increases flow to 4L NC. Current vitals: RR 32, HR 108, BP 142/88, Temp 38.2°C. He is using accessory muscles and speaking only in 3-4 word sentences. ABG reveals: pH 7.28, PaCO2 74 mmHg, PaO2 46 mmHg, HCO3 34 mEq/L — consistent with acute-on-chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure. Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms and no new infiltrate. Peak expiratory flow is 38% of predicted. Physical exam reveals prolonged expiratory phase, diffuse expiratory wheezes, and diminished air entry bilaterally. The patient is anxious but arousable and following commands.

title: Major Burns: Airway Management and Parkland Formula Fluid Resuscitation
scenario: A 34-year-old male, weighing 80 kg, is transported by EMS to the emergency department following entrapment in a residential house fire. The incident occurred approximately 90 minutes ago. Burns are noted to the anterior chest (9% TBSA), entire right arm (9% TBSA), and anterior right leg (9% TBSA), totaling 27% TBSA with a combination of partial-thickness and full-thickness injuries. The patient has singed nasal hairs, hoarse voice, facial burns, and is producing carbonaceous (sooty) sputum — all hallmark signs of inhalation injury. He is alert but anxious, speaking in short phrases, and reports pain at the burn sites. Vital signs on arrival: BP 108/72 mmHg, HR 128 bpm, RR 24 breaths/min, SpO2 93% on 15L/min via non-rebreather mask, Temp 35.8°C (hypothermic). No IV access has been established. The patient is still wearing fire-damaged clothing. The nurse recognizes this as a complex, life-threatening emergency requiring simultaneous airway protection, fluid resuscitation, hypothermia prevention, and wound management. Prioritization of interventions is critical as edema from inhalation injury may rapidly progress to complete airway obstruction.

title: Sickle Cell Vaso-Occlusive Crisis with Acute Chest Syndrome
scenario: A 24-year-old female with known sickle cell disease (HbSS genotype) is admitted to the medical-surgical unit for a vaso-occlusive crisis (VOC). The crisis was triggered by dehydration following a 3-day gastrointestinal illness with vomiting and poor oral intake. She reports severe bilateral leg pain rated 10/10, describing it as 'worse than usual.' On admission, vital signs are: BP 108/64 mmHg, HR 118 bpm, RR 22 breaths/min, Temperature 38.4°C, SpO2 91% on room air. Laboratory results show Hgb 6.2 g/dL (her baseline is 7.5 g/dL), Hct 19%, reticulocytes 18%, WBC 14,200/µL, total bilirubin 3.8 mg/dL (indirect 2.9 mg/dL), and elevated LDH — consistent with ongoing hemolysis. Over the first hour of admission, the nurse notes the patient's respiratory rate is increasing. The patient now reports new chest pain and a productive cough. A chest X-ray reveals a new right lower lobe infiltrate, which may represent infarction or infection. SpO2 has dropped to 88% on room air. The clinical picture is rapidly evolving and requires urgent reassessment and intervention.

title: Massive Pulmonary Embolism with Hemodynamic Compromise
scenario: A 52-year-old female presents on postoperative day 3 following a right total knee replacement. She was receiving enoxaparin 40mg SQ daily for prophylaxis. At 1200, she develops sudden onset dyspnea, right-sided pleuritic chest pain, and intense anxiety. Her morning vitals at 0600 were BP 128/80, HR 88, SpO2 96% on room air. Current vitals: BP 88/52 (down from 102/68 one hour ago), HR 126 (sinus tachycardia), RR 30, SpO2 88% on room air, Temp 37.4°C. Assessment reveals the right calf is warm, tender, and swollen — 3 cm larger in circumference than the left. Wells Score for PE is 7, indicating high clinical probability. ABG results: pH 7.48, PaCO2 28 mmHg, PaO2 54 mmHg — consistent with acute respiratory alkalosis and hypoxemia. EKG shows sinus tachycardia with an S1Q3T3 pattern and signs of new right heart strain. CT Pulmonary Angiography (CT-PA) has been ordered but results are pending. Despite administration of 2L of IV normal saline, blood pressure has not improved. The clinical picture is consistent with massive (high-risk) pulmonary embolism with hemodynamic compromise requiring immediate intervention.
